rights_tasks.task: Review the Extractor's output and identify the contract type, parties, and their roles (grantor vs. grantee). Determine the intended exploitation: what does the grantee plan to do with these rights?

Analyze the grant of rights for scope sufficiency: Map each specific right granted (reproduce, distribute, perform, display, synchronize, make available, exploit) against the intended exploitation Identify any specific rights that would be needed for the intended exploitation but are not explicitly granted Classify the grant as broad (all rights necessary for full exploitation), narrow (limited to specific uses), or mixed Identify rights that fall in the "silent zone" — neither explicitly granted nor explicitly reserved

Analyze territory scope: List all territories explicitly granted Identify any territory restrictions or carve-outs Assess whether the territory grant is sufficient for the grantee's likely distribution footprint For multi-territory grants, verify that language rights align with each territory's primary languages Flag any territories that may be subject to sanctions or export controls (for cross_reference to Regulatory Analyst)

Analyze platform and media scope: List all licensed media and platforms explicitly granted Identify any media/platform restrictions or carve-outs Assess whether emerging platforms (FAST, social media, interactive) are covered or fall in the silent zone Evaluate whether the media grant language is future-proof or technology-specific (e.g., "SVOD" vs. "all digital distribution means now known or hereafter devised")

Analyze language, dubbing, and subtitle rights: Are dubbing rights explicitly granted? For which languages? Are subtitling rights explicitly granted? For which languages? Who bears localization costs? Is this a recoupable expense? (Cross_reference to Financial Analyst if economic implications are material) Who approves the quality of localized versions? What is the approval mechanic? Who owns the dubbed/subtitled versions upon termination? For multi-territory grants, are there territories where the grant exists but no corresponding language rights have been granted?

Analyze chain of title and IP ownership: Identify the party represented as the underlying IP owner Classify the chain of title warranty by strength: "owns all rights" (strongest) → "has the right to grant" (moderate) → "to the best of its knowledge" (weakest) → absent (no warranty) Evaluate any work-for-hire references: is the classification documented? Is a signed written agreement referenced? Identify any disclosed prior encumbrances, liens, or existing licenses. Assess compatibility with the new grant. Identify any disclosed title disputes or pending claims If the contract is entirely silent on chain of title, flag as a significant gap

Analyze music licensing completeness: Sync rights: are synchronization licenses for all compositions addressed? Status (cleared, pending, to be obtained)? Master use rights: are master use licenses for all recordings addressed? Status? Public performance rights: are PRO affiliations identified (ASCAP, BMI, SESAC, GMR)? Is performance rights clearance addressed? For each leg (sync, master, performance): is it confirmed cleared, warranted as cleared, acknowledged as pending, or not addressed? Are music clearance territories and media aligned with the overall distribution grant? Are there MFN provisions across music cues? (Cross_reference to Financial Analyst for economic impact)

Analyze embedded IP clearances: Does the contract warrant that all third-party IP embedded in the content (logos, footage, artwork, brand marks, archival material) is cleared? How specific is the warranty? General ("all necessary clearances obtained") vs. specific ("clearances for the materials listed in Exhibit C")? Are any specific embedded IP items identified as uncleared or pending? Is there indemnification backing the clearance warranty?

Analyze sublicensing and assignment provisions: Can the grantee sublicense? Under what conditions (unlimited, with approval, prohibited)? If sublicensing is permitted with approval, what is the approval mechanic? Is silence treated as consent or refusal? Does the grantee remain liable for sublicensee obligations? Can the agreement be assigned? Under what conditions? Is there a change-of-control provision that triggers consent requirements?

Analyze reservation of rights and exclusivity: What rights has the grantor explicitly reserved? Are there material rights in the silent zone — neither granted nor reserved? Is the grant exclusive or non-exclusive? If exclusive, for which territories, media, and time periods? Analyze holdback provisions: what is the grantor restricted from licensing during the holdback period? Are there gaps in holdback coverage that allow competing exploitation? Evaluate holdback duration against typical windowing for this contract type

Analyze reversion mechanics from a rights perspective: When do rights revert — on expiration, on termination, or both? What happens to sublicenses upon reversion? Do they survive, terminate, or require individual negotiation? Is there a sell-off period? What exploitation is permitted during sell-off? What materials must be returned or destroyed upon reversion? Who owns derivative works (dubbed versions, marketing materials, metadata) upon reversion?

Assess E&O insurability: Based on the chain of title provisions, clearance warranties, and embedded IP provisions, would an E&O insurer likely have concerns? Are the insurance provisions in the contract (coverage amounts, additional insured, proof of coverage) adequate for this deal type? If insurance is not addressed, flag as a gap for this contract type

Assess digital and emerging rights from a rights/clearance perspective: Are AI training rights addressed? If content could be used as ML training data without explicit consent, flag as a rights gap Are NFT, metaverse, and interactive media rights addressed or in the silent zone? Are social media clip rights explicitly scoped or are they ambiguous? For digital likeness provisions, are the clearance mechanics sufficient — is consent per-use, blanket, or undefined?

Produce a rights and clearance findings summary: List all rights gaps identified: specific rights, territories, media, or languages where exploitation may be blocked or contested List all clearance risks: pending clearances, weak warranties, undocumented chain of title elements List all silent zone items: material rights neither granted nor reserved Assess overall rights posture: clean (minimal gaps), conditional (gaps exist but are manageable), or deficient (material gaps that block exploitation) Note any cross-references to other specialist agents
Populate metadata and run verification before finalizing output.
